3 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:物部二田塩 up to rev 87662902
revision.text:
'''物部二田 塩'''（もののべのふつた の しお）は、[[飛鳥時代]]の[[豪族]]。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[造]]。
== 系譜 == 物部二田氏は、[[筑前国]][[鞍手郡]]二田郷（現在の[[福岡県]]鞍手郡[[鞍手町]]）を本拠とする伴造氏族で、「物部」は[[伴部]]の[[物部]]であり、二田は本拠地の地名によるものである。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』未定雑姓、右京によると、「二田物部（ふたたのもののべ）。[[ニギハヤヒ|神饒速日]]天降之時従者。二田天物部之後也」とあり、『[[先代旧事本紀]]』天神本紀によると、「五部の造が供領（とものみやつこ）となり、天物部(あまのもののべ)を率いて天降りお仕えした」とあり、その中の一つに「二田造（ふただのみやつこ）」の名が挙げられており、さらに、「天物部ら二十五部の人が、同じく兵杖を帯びて天降り、お仕えした」とあって、その筆頭に「二田物部（ふただのもののべ）」の名が挙げられている。
== 記録 == 物部二田塩の名前は、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第二十五の以下の箇所にのみ現れる。
[[大化]]5年（[[649年]]）3月、[[蘇我日向]]の讒言により、[[蘇我倉山田石川麻呂]]は一族とともに山田寺の仏殿で自殺した<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化5年3月25日条</ref>。この時、木麻呂・[[蘇我日向]]・[[穂積咋]]らに率いられた追討軍が山田寺を包囲したが、塩を召して、既に自決している石川麻呂の首を斬らせたという。
{{quotation|是に、二田塩<small>（ふつたのしほ）</small>、仍<small>（すなはち）</small>ち大刀<small>（たち）</small>を抜きて、其の宍<small>（しし）</small>を刺し挙げて、叱咤<small>（たけ）</small>び啼叫<small>（さけ）</small>びて、始<small>（いま）</small>し斬りつ<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化5年3月26日条</ref>}}
この有様を耳にした[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]の妃であった蘇我造媛（[[遠智娘]]）は悲しみもだえ、「塩」という名前を聞くことを嫌い、そのため近臣のものたちは「塩」のことを堅塩（きたし）と呼ぶようになった。造媛はこのことが原因でなくなってしまった<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化5年3月是月条</ref>。
== 考察 == 斬首の際の塩の仕業は、伴部としての物部が処刑を行う際の作法を表していると、[[平野卓治]]は述べている<ref>『別冊歴史読本 古代人物総覧』より「2豪族」p212、[[新人物往来社]]、1996年</ref>。
また、塩の行った残虐な行為の背景には、孝徳天皇が大化元年8月に東国[[国司]]を任命し<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化元年8月5日条</ref>、翌年3月にその中の一人であった穂積咋が勤務状況を叱責されているところから<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化2年3月19日条</ref>、「改新」派への反「改新」派側からの反発の意味もあったのではないか、と[[篠川賢]]は述べている<ref>『別冊歴史読本 古代人物総覧』より「石川麻呂滅亡事件」</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（四）、[[岩波文庫]]、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀（下）』、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 『別冊歴史読本 古代人物総覧』、[[新人物往来社]]、1996年 * 『蘇我氏の古代史 謎の一族はなぜ滅びたのか』、[[武光誠]]、[[平凡社]]新書、2008年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編、[[雄山閣]]、2015年
== 関連項目 == * [[二田城]] {{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もののへのふつたの しお}} [[Category:物部氏|ふつたの しお]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

また、塩の行った残虐な行為の背景には、孝徳天皇が大化元年8月に東国[[国司]]を任命し<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化元年8月5日条</ref>、翌年3月にその中の一人であった穂積咋が勤務状況を叱責されているところから<ref>『日本書紀』孝徳天皇大化2年3月19日条</ref>、「改新」派への反「改新」派側からの反発の意味もあったのではないか、と[[篠川賢]]は述べている<ref>『別冊歴史読本 古代人物総覧』より「石川麻呂滅亡事件」</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（四）、[[岩波文庫]]、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀（下）』、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 『別冊歴史読本 古代人物総覧』、[[新人物往来社]]、1996年 * 『蘇我氏の古代史 謎の一族はなぜ滅びたのか』、[[武光誠]]、[[平凡社]]新書、2008年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編、[[雄山閣]]、2015年
== 関連項目 == * [[二田城]] {{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もののへのふつたの しお}} [[Category:物部氏|ふつたの しお]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
'''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[Kurate]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigi Hayahi no Mikoto]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[Soga no Kurayamada Ishikawa Maro]].
In [[Taika]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Asuka period people
revision.text:
'''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[Kurate]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigi Hayahi no Mikoto]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[Soga no Kurayamada Ishikawa Maro]].
In [[Taika]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigi Hayahi no Mikoto]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[Soga no Kurayamada Ishikawa Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q86745175]] Wikidata name: Mononobenofutsuta no Shio [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:物部二田塩]] {{Infobox person}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q86745175]] Wikidata name: Mononobenofutsuta no Shio [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:物部二田塩]] {{Infobox person}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q86745175]] Wikidata name: Mononobenofutsuta no Shio [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>

Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>

His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.

== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:物部二田塩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:物部二田塩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:物部二田塩]] {{Infobox person}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q86745175]] Wikidata name: Mononobenofutsuta no Shio [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mononobe no Fututa no Shio''' (物部二田 塩) was a member of the [[Mononobe clan]], a noble family of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]].
In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
{{people-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|物部二田塩|version=87662902|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]] New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.

His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|en|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}.
In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical Figures of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}} New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.

His kabane (hereditary title) was [[Zō (造)]].
== Genealogy == The Mononobe no Fututa clan originated in [[Fututa-no-go]] in [[Kurate District]], {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day [[:en:Kurate|Kurate]], {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). As part of the Mononobe lineage, their name derives from their function as [[:en:Tomobe|Tomobe]] (servitors) under the Mononobe structure.
According to the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', under "Miscellaneous Surnames of the Right Capital," Fututa Mononobe (二田物部) are said to be descendants of [[Nigihayahi]]’s attendants who accompanied him during his descent to earth. Similarly, the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' identifies the "Fututa clan" as one of the original groups serving as imperial guards under the celestial Mononobe structure, highlighting their martial legacy.
== Historical Record == Mononobe no Fututa no Shio is mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Volume 25, in the context of the downfall of {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|en|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|1=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}.
In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)|1=Taika (era)}} 5 (649 CE), under the influence of [[Soga no Hinokuma]], an order was issued to execute Soga no Ishikawa Maro, who had taken refuge in [[Yamadadera]]. After his suicide, Shio was summoned to decapitate the deceased Ishikawa Maro.
{{quotation|Fututa no Shio drew his sword, thrust it into the flesh, lifted it high, and, while crying out fiercely, delivered the decapitation.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, 26th day</ref>}}
The act deeply disturbed [[Princess Ōtome]] (Soga no Otome), consort of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], who mourned the event bitterly. She loathed hearing Shio’s name and demanded it be replaced with "Kitashio" (堅塩). Her grief reportedly contributed to her untimely death.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Kōtoku, Taika 5, 3rd month, recorded events</ref>
== Analysis == The execution method performed by Shio reflects the Mononobe clan’s traditional role as the imperial executioners, according to [[Takuharu Hirano]].<ref>''Historical People of Ancient Japan,'' p. 212, Shimbun Ōraisha, 1996</ref>
Additionally, the brutal manner of Shio’s actions may symbolize political resistance from anti-reform factions to Emperor Kōtoku’s policies during the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]]. [[Ken Sasakawa]] notes the tensions between the pro- and anti-reform factions as potential background for Shio’s involvement.<ref>''Historical People of Ancient Japan,'' "Ishikawa Maro’s Downfall Incident"</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 4), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995. * [[Masao Ujitani]], ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume 2), [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988. * ''Historical People of Ancient Japan,'' [[Shimbun Ōraisha]], 1996. * ''The History of the Soga Clan,'' [[Makoto Takemitsu]], {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}} New Edition, 2008. * ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans'' (New Edition), [[Saeki Ariyoshi]], [[Yuzankaku]], 2015.
== See Also == * [[Fututa Castle]]
